coredata.dc:title: Are food-derived carbon dots really edible? A potential harm versus potential benefits analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Malchenko, Vitalina A.
Tracey, Chantal T.
Krivoshapkina, Elena F.
Nazarova, Elena A.
coredata.dc:description:
Background Carbon dots exhibit good antimicrobial, antioxidant, and optical properties that the food industry can utilise to prevent food spoilage as well as monitor food quality and safety. Conventionally, carbon dots have been prevented from being in direct contact with foodstuff because they are considered harmful in large quantities, especially those derived from toxic inorganic carbon sources; as a result, their consumption is ill-advised. Recently, however, it has been proposed that non-toxic carbon dots can be synthesised from biopolymers and biomolecules found in food, and that these food-derived carbon-based nanoparticles are safe to consume.
Scope and approach In this commentary, we explore the arguments both for and against the consumption of food-derived carbon dots. We also investigate the validity of claims suggesting these carbon dots are harmless as well as the potential harms associated with their consumption.
Key findings and conclusions While some research suggests that carbon dots obtained from food sources are not only harmless but may have beneficial effects on gut health, a greater number of studies indicate that these food-derived carbon dots may be just as harmful as regular carbon dots. However, their usefulness in food quality and safety control remains invaluable. Thus, until indisputable evidence regarding their safety is made available, we propose a potential solution to their safe and responsible use in the food industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanoparticles
Active packaging
Intelligent packaging
Edible coatings
Food quality assurance
Food safety assurance

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst misctext orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Are food-derived carbon dots really safe to consume?

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Russian Science Foundation Grant \u2116 24-76-10093 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Publication trends showing the relationship between carbon dot safety and their incorporation in food packaging. As shown on the left, there is a corresponding increase in the incorporation of carbon dots in food packaging as information available on the safety of carbon dots and their synthesis from food sources becomes more available. As shown on the right, the application of carbon dots in food quality and safety control is becoming more widespread.

Comparison of some physicochemical characteristics of carbon dots made from inorganic carbon sources and those made from food sources. Carbon dots (C-dots) obtained from inorganic carbon sources like graphene, graphite, and carbon nanotubes, which tend to be highly toxic, are not recommended for consumption. When synthesised from organic sources, primarily biomolecules and biopolymers found in food that are proven safe and non-toxic, carbon dots are considered similarly safe and, therefore, consumable.

Some applications of carbon dots in active and intelligent food packaging. Fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) exhibit strong antioxidant and antimicrobial properties, which can be used to actively extend the shelf life of foodstuff by eliminating dangerous pathogens and neutralising harmful free radicals (left). Carbon dots are also used to detect spoilage indicators, adulterants, and contaminants in food via fluorescence quenching, which can be used to monitor food quality and safety (right).

Potential adverse and beneficial effects of carbon dots obtained from food on the human body. Some studies show that food carbon dots (i.e., both foodborne carbon dots and food-derived carbon dots) can disrupt metabolism, damage the gut, bioaccumulate, and trigger immune responses (left). Other studies, however, suggest that food-derived carbon dots can have beneficial effects on the digestive system and alleviate symptoms caused by common digestive diseases (right).

Some nanoparticles commonly incorporated in food packaging, their roles in food packaging, their effect on zebrafish embryo during toxicity testing, and their minimum toxic concentrations. Compared to other nanoparticles, carbon dots obtained from food sources, like fruits, have significantly higher minimum toxic concentrations, indicating their relative safety.

list-item.para: Decreased glutathione peroxidase and catalase activity
Increased HIF4 and Pxmp2 mRNA expression
Improve water and gas barrier properties
Increased superoxide dismutase, catalase, and glutathione S transferase activity

row.entry: Carbon quantum dots derived from graphite rods

simple-head.ce:title: Are food-derived carbon dots really edible? A potential harm versus potential benefits analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BioNanoTech Laboratory, ITMO University, 9 Lomonosova Street, St. Petersburg, 191002, Russian Federation
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BioNanoTech Laboratory, ITMO University, 9 Lomonosova Street, St. Petersburg, 191002, Russian Federation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots exhibit good antimicrobial, antioxidant, and optical properties that the food industry can utilise to prevent food spoilage as well as monitor food quality and safety. Conventionally, carbon dots have been prevented from being in direct contact with foodstuff because they are considered harmful in large quantities, especially those derived from toxic inorganic carbon sources; as a result, their consumption is ill-advised. Recently, however, it has been proposed that non-toxic carbon dots can be synthesised from biopolymers and biomolecules found in food, and that these food-derived carbon-based nanoparticles are safe to consume.

In this commentary, we explore the arguments both for and against the consumption of food-derived carbon dots. We also investigate the validity of claims suggesting these carbon dots are harmless as well as the potential harms associated with their consumption.

While some research suggests that carbon dots obtained from food sources are not only harmless but may have beneficial effects on gut health, a greater number of studies indicate that these food-derived carbon dots may be just as harmful as regular carbon dots. However, their usefulness in food quality and safety control remains invaluable. Thus, until indisputable evidence regarding their safety is made available, we propose a potential solution to their safe and responsible use in the food industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The food and beverage sector has been aware of the physicochemical characteristics of carbon dots for the past two decades. First, carbon dots are well-known for their optical properties: brightly fluorescent upon irradiation with ultraviolet or visible light, these carbon-based nanoparticles form non-fluorescent complexes in the presence of several food spoilage indicators, adulterants, and contaminants (Gupta et al., 2023). Additionally, carbon dots exhibit commendable antimicrobial and antioxidant properties, making them suitable for the inhibition and eradication of harmful pathogens as well as the neutralisation of dangerous free radicals that can lead to food spoilage (C. Liu et al., 2024). To capitalise on these useful characteristics, the food industry has explored the potential utilisation of carbon dots to extend the shelf life of foodstuff and in food quality and safety control (Manzoor et al., 2023).

Carbon dots are just one type of carbon-based nanoparticles. Due to justifiable safety concerns surrounding their potential migration into foods and beverages, carbon dots have traditionally been prevented from being in direct contact with foodstuff. This is because, due to their size, carbon dots can cross cellular barriers and access areas of the body that larger particles cannot. This allows them to interact with the body at the cellular level, making them an inherent health risk (Encinas-Gimenez et al., 2024). Furthermore, the physicochemical properties of nanoparticles can be altered as they pass through the gastrointestinal tract after digestion, rendering them potentially toxic.

As such, carbon dots have been incorporated into small devices or indicators with which samples removed from the bulk can be tested and then discarded (Gupta et al., 2023). For example, carbon dots have been used in colorimetric and fluorescent probes and biosensors designed to detect physical, chemical, and biological contaminants as well as in electrochemical sensors that have been used to monitor essential nutrients (Huang et al., 2022; Lei et al., 2025; Sun et al., 2024). In these systems, the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots is used to indicate whether or not food is suitable for consumption. Such systems may be included on the inside or outside of food packaging to create intelligent food packaging that monitor the state of the food, the environment surrounding the food, and interactions between the food and its environment. While useful, such systems only provide information on the state of the food rather than actively hindering spoilage.

Packaging that modifies the condition of the packed food by interacting with the product and its environment is known as active packaging. Concerning carbon dots, active food packaging utilises their antimicrobial and antioxidant properties. For carbon dots to be effective antioxidants and antimicrobial agents, they need to be in direct contact with foodstuff. For example, carbon dots have been incorporated into edible food coatings designed to extend the shelf life of fruits and vegetables and added to soy milk as an antimicrobial agent (Priyadarshi et al., 2024; L. Zhao et al., 2023). However, as stated earlier, the potential consumption of carbon dots is somewhat controversial.

It has been postulated that carbon dots will take on the properties of their precursors; thus, if biocompatible, non-toxic precursors are used, the resulting carbon dots should also be biocompatible and non-toxic. Since then, biopolymers and biomolecules, which are natural polymers and molecules produced by living organisms, have become very popular feedstock – especially those obtained from food sources (Gupta et al., 2023; C. Liu et al., 2024; Manzoor et al., 2023). The carbon dots obtained from these sources have been shown to be significantly less toxic than other nanoparticles; thus, theoretically, they can be considered ‘food-safe’. The idea of ‘food-safe’ carbon dots has gained significant traction. Publication statistics from Web of Science show that as the number of studies on the safety of carbon dots has increased, there has been a corresponding increase in the number of studies dedicated to obtaining carbon dots from food sources as well as the application of carbon dots in food packaging (Fig. 1 , left). There has also been a noticeable increase in the number of articles regarding the use of carbon dots in food safety and quality control (Fig. 1, right).

In this commentary, we discuss the increasing use of carbon dots in food safety and quality control. In the second section, we describe how carbon source and synthesis methods affect the physicochemical properties of carbon dots, which in turn influence their biodegradability and toxicity. In section three, we investigate the safety claims surrounding carbon dots obtained from food sources. In section four, we discuss the effects of carbon dots on living organisms in an effort to determine whether or not these carbon-based nanoparticles are indeed safe to consume. Finally, we offer our perspectives on how the food industry can responsibly exploit the useful properties of carbon dots in food safety and quality assurance while maintaining human and environmental health.

Carbon dots are zero-dimensional quasi-spherical carbon-based structures that are usually less than 10 nm in size. These nanoparticles generally have a core-shell structure: the core consists of sp2 and sp3 hybridised carbon atoms while the shell is comprised of various surface functional groups or polymer chains attached to the core. Although primarily carbon, the core can also contain heteroatoms like nitrogen, boron, sulphur, and various metal ions, which are added during synthesis to improve the intrinsic physical and chemical properties of carbon dots by changing the electronic structure. The shell generally consists of oxygen-rich functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups; however, functional groups containing nitrogen, sulphur, and phosphorous can be added during or after synthesis (Tracey et al., 2024). Both the carbon source and synthesis method used influence the physicochemical properties of carbon dots.

Carbon dots are generally made from the top-down by cleaving large inorganic graphitic structures using harsh synthesis methods like oxidative cutting, laser ablation, electrochemical exfoliation, and arc discharge or constructed from the bottom-up using small organic molecules under mild synthesis conditions such as pyrolysis, microwave-assisted synthesis, and solvothermal synthesis using harmless solvents like water, ethanol, and acetic acid. Top-down carbon dots are obtained by chemically or physically cutting large inorganic carbon frameworks into smaller fragments. While top-down synthetic approaches tend to be fast, efficient, and produce high-quality end products, they often have high energy consumption and require the use of expensive equipment and reagents as well as toxic organic chemicals (Tracey et al., 2024).

Top-down carbon dots often have multilayered crystalline structures and properties very similar to that of the initial carbon source (Ozyurt et al., 2023; Sharma & Das, 2019). The most-often used inorganic carbon sources, namely graphene, graphite, and their derivatives (Fig. 2 , left), are highly toxic and can accumulate in the liver, lungs, and blood vessels; cause vomiting and stomach aches due to bowel obstruction; cause cell death; and damage cellular DNA if ingested (Svadlakova et al., 2022); therefore, carbon dots obtained from such sources are likely to have similar adverse health effects. However, data on the biocompatibility and non-toxicity of carbon dots made from these sources remains inconclusive and their consumption is ill-advised until more conclusive research on any confirmed health effects is made available (Manzoor et al., 2023; Svadlakova et al., 2022).

Bottom-up synthesis methods, however, can be used for the production of less toxic dots from a wide variety of organic sources, such as proteins, glucose, sucrose, citric acid, and ascorbic acid (Fig. 2, right). Produced via the ionisation, dissociation, evaporation, or sublimation then subsequent condensation of smaller carbon units, bottom-up carbon dots generally have amorphous structures dissimilar to that of the initial carbon source (Sharma & Das, 2019). In addition to being more economical and ecological than top-down production methods, microwave-assisted synthesis offers several other advantages, including simple experimental setup, a broad selection of viable precursors and solvents, fast reaction times, and high carbon dot yield; however, this synthesis method requires specialised equipment, has low scalability, and tends to produce carbon dots with non-uniform particle size (Cui et al., 2021). While solvothermal synthesis, which includes hydrothermal synthesis if the solvent used is water, allows more control over the composition, size, shape, and surface properties of carbon dots and the resulting product has high purity, high biocompatibility, and low toxicity, this method requires high temperature and pressure; has long reaction times; and requires intricate, prolonged product purification processes (Cui et al., 2021). Also known as carbonisation, pyrolysis is a low-cost, large-scale, solvent-free method for producing carbon dots with low toxicity and good biocompatibility; however, carbon dots prepared using this method have limited applicability due to their low fluorescence quantum yields (Marpongahtun et al., 2023).

Thanks to the permeating idea of biocompatible carbon dots, researchers have proposed that these nanoparticles can be in direct contact with or even directly incorporated into foodstuff. Several studies in the literature describe incorporating carbon dots into foods as preservatives to extend the shelf life of products as well as into edible coatings designed to actively prevent food spoilage or indicate the presence of spoilage indicators, contaminants, and adulterants (Fig. 3 ) (Priyadarshi et al., 2024; L. Zhao et al., 2023). As the literature has been showing lately, some researchers believe that consuming carbon dots obtained from food sources is a completely safe practice.

Some studies do seem to support this hypothesis, with carbon dots synthesised from biopolymers being harmless to highly sensitive zebrafish embryos at concentrations of up to 1500 μg/mL (Dias et al., 2019). Zebrafish embryos are often used in toxicology studies to gain insight into the potential detrimental effects harmful pharmaceutical and environmental substances may have on organisms (Chakraborty et al., 2016). For comparison, other nanoparticles are toxic to zebrafish embryos at concentrations as low as 0.25 μg/mL, and can lead to unwanted outcomes like premature hatching, changes in gene expression, and developmental abnormalities (Qiang et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2019). Table 1 shows the adverse effects of various nanoparticles on zebrafish embryos and their minimum toxic concentrations. Thus, as shown, food-derived carbon dots can be considered significantly safer than other nanoparticles used in the food industry. Interestingly, however, burgeoning research seems to contradict this stance.

Foodborne carbon dots, which are carbon nanostructures formed through the spontaneous self-organization of partially burned lipids, proteins, and other food molecules with additives during high-temperature cooking or baking, can be found in a wide variety of food products (Zhang et al., 2023, 2024). Like food-derived carbon dots, foodborne carbon dots were initially considered safe, especially since they are an unavoidable part of the food-making process. However, increasingly more emerging studies have indicated that this may not be true and that foodborne carbon dots are potentially cytotoxic, adversely affect the immune system, and can bioaccumulate in cells upon ingestion, which has resulted in foodborne carbon dots being reclassified as a new type of nanocontaminant (Zhang et al., 2023, 2024).

According to experiments on mice, foodborne carbon dots have been shown to decrease the number of beneficial bacteria found in the gut (e.g., Coprococcus, Bacteroides, and Muribaculaceae), leading to an increase in the number of harmful, pro-inflammatory bacteria (e.g., Proteobacteria, Desulfovibrionaceae, and Ruminococcaceae) (Zhang et al., 2023). This imbalance can result in damage the intestinal mucus layer and induce hepatic insulin resistance. Furthermore, the pro-inflammatory bacteria can release the endotoxin lipopolysaccharide, which can lead to inflammation that extends beyond the intestines (Zhang et al., 2023, 2024). Foodborne carbon dots have also been shown to aggravate high-fat-diet-induced glucose homeostasis by disrupting the gut-liver axis; affect lipid metabolism; induce lysosome-dependent cell death; and affect the male reproductive system (Fig. 4 , left).

Foodborne carbon dots have also been found to induce inflammatory responses in human blood cultures at concentrations as low as 500 μg/mL, increasing the levels of humoral immune cytokine interleukin (Lategan et al., 2018), which may lead to hypersensitivity and autoimmune reactions. Studies have shown that carbon-based nanoparticles can cross the blood-brain barrier, which facilitates active interactions between the bloodstream and the central nervous system, and in vitro testing shows that foodborne carbon dots are actively absorbed by human hepatocarcinoma cells and have the propensity to accumulate in the cytoplasm (Manzoor et al., 2023). Foodborne carbon dots also can penetrate the cell nucleus and tend to accumulate there in very high concentrations. Thus, it can be hypothesised that they may impact cellular DNA, potentially resulting in genetic alterations. In vitro experiments show that foodborne carbon dots have a moderately toxic effect on human mesenchymal stem cells at concentrations of approximately 400 μg/mL and affects mitochondrial transmembrane potentials; increases intracellular reactive oxygen species levels; and decreases the levels of mRNA transcripts of genes responding to metabolic stress, such as protein p53, which acts as a suppressor of malignant tumour formation (Al-Hadi et al., 2016).

This also raises important concerns regarding the consumption of foodborne carbon dots during pregnancy and whether it may pose negative consequences for both the mother and the developing foetus (Fig. 4, left). Genetic changes induced by nanoparticle exposure could affect embryonic development, leading to an increased risk of anomalies and organ developmental disorders (Chung et al., 2021; Sima et al., 2020). Furthermore, the potential impact on the placental barrier must be considered, as nanoparticles may cause damage or alterations that compromise its integrity. This could result in the placenta losing its protective barrier function, allowing a greater influx of toxins, harmful substances, bacteria, and viruses into the foetal environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Are food-derived carbon dots really safe to consume?
ce:section.ce:para: If foodborne carbon dots, which are an unintentional and unpreventable byproduct of the food manufacturing process, are today considered harmful, how are carbon dots intentionally synthesised from food products not? Why is being proposed that it is safe to incorporate food-derived carbon dots into items designed for consumption? Is it a question of bioaccumulation? It is true that most of the concerning results regarding foodborne carbon dots were observed in mice rather than larger mammalian organisms like human beings; however, researchers are proposing incorporating food-derived carbon dots into edible food coating for fruits and vegetables as well as mixing them in with (soy) milk to extend its shelf life – products that many people consume (almost) daily (Priyadarshi et al., 2024; L. Zhao et al., 2023). Some studies have suggested that carbon dots take as little as 72 h to be completely excreted from the body (Chung et al., 2021); however, the chances of bioaccumulation and the development of adverse health effects are indeed possible if replenishment is (near) constant. While the majority of conducted studies investigated the effects of foodborne carbon dots, others have shown that these harmful properties also apply to carbon dots derived from biopolymers and biomolecules found in food.

For example, a study that investigated the in vitro and in vivo cytotoxicities of carbon dots showed that carbon dots with many oxygen- and nitrogen-containing surface functional groups (e.g., carboxyl and nitric groups) might be toxic with daily dosing (Kuznietsova et al., 2023). Carbon dots are often doped with nitrogen during synthesis to increase their photoluminescence quantum yields and, therefore, their functional applications, which results in the formation of multiple nitrogen-containing groups on the carbon dot surface (Tracey et al., 2024). The study also showed that daily exposure to carbon dots at a dosage of 5 mg/kg of body mass for 14 days had a lethality rate of up to 50 % in mice (Kuznietsova et al., 2023). While not substantial, the kidneys, livers, and spleens of the surviving mice did experience some damage. Due to their anatomical, physiological, and genetic similarities to humans, mice are considered very accurate predictors for human health indicators. Furthermore, because of their short reproductive cycles and their ability to be tested in large numbers, mice models can provide statistically significant data.

The same study also evaluated the cytotoxicity of the prepared carbon dots against human lung cancer A549 cells and found that they inhibited cell growth and showed some cytotoxic activity (Kuznietsova et al., 2023). It can be argued that carbon dots showing anti-cancerous activity might be useful; however, in another study, while carbon dots also synthesised from glucose were found to be toxic against cancerous cells HeLa and HepG2 human cells, they were also toxic against noncancerous HEK-293 human cells (Y.-Y. Liu et al., 2021). Thus, the authors recommended limited use of carbon dots since they are toxic to both unhealthy and healthy cells.

In another example, Xiao et al. (2016) studied the toxic effects of carbon dots obtained from citric acid and betaine hydrochloride (the acidic form of betaine, which is found in foods such as beets, broccoli, grains, shellfish, and spinach) on the embryos of a rare minnow species at different stages of development (Xiao et al., 2016). The study showed that exposure to these carbon dots can cause substantial damage to the DNA of embryonic cells, which may be due to induced oxidative stress. In another more recent example, carbon dots synthesised from ε-poly-L-lysine, a naturally occurring poly(amino acid) that is sometimes used as a food preservative, induce pathological damage to the intestines by disrupting natural intestinal microflora and causing inflammation (Jia et al., 2023). In vitro experiments showed that food-derived carbon dots limit the activity of intestinal epithelial cells by reducing the expression of ZO-1 and occludin (Jia et al., 2023). ZO-1 and occludin are vital membrane proteins that are necessary for the formation and maintenance of tight junctions in epithelial cells as well as the regulation of paracellular permeability and cell polarity (Kuo et al., 2022). The study found that the suppression of these factors can result in intestinal epithelial damage.

As shown, carbon dots pose a significant threat to human health as exposure to these nanoparticles can damage DNA, cause cell death and apoptosis, and induce inflammatory responses. This is because carbon dots induce oxidative stress, which occurs when an organism cannot readily remove harmful reactive oxygen species and any intermediates due to an antioxidant deficit in the system. Some studies have shown that carbon dots exhibit oxidant activity while others highlight the antioxidant activity of these nanoparticles; however, whether carbon dots display antioxidant or oxidant activity depends on the carbon source and synthesis method (Innocenzi & Stagi, 2023).

Carbon dots exhibit size-dependent cytotoxicity because they can enter cells and cellular structures. Carbon dots that display oxidant activity increase the number of free radicals within the cell and trigger cellular responses such as mitochondrial respiration, NP-cell interactions, and immune cell activation, which results in reactive oxygen species-mediated damage (Kumah et al., 2023). Carbon dots undergo structural changes as they pass through the gastrointestinal tract, which leads to changes in their physicochemical properties. This, in turn, changes the biological activity of carbon dots, potentially inducing their generation of reactive oxygen species, which is one of the greatest contributors to nanoparticle toxicity (Manke et al., 2013). The degree of cytotoxicity, however, depends on several other factors, namely carbon dot composition, the types of functional groups present on the carbon dot surface, and the degree of crystallinity of the carbon core.

Carbon dots synthesised from foods have other cellular-level effects on organisms. Evidence has been presented that food-derived carbon dots can influence the activity of NF-κB and other inflammatory mediators. However, the available data is also contradictory; although one study has shown that carbon dots obtained from garlic can help reduce inflammation, another study claims that carbon dots derived from malting barley causes inflammation (Kalkal et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022). These nanoparticles have also been shown to affect metabolic function. Studies indicate that carbon dots affect the function of important glycolysis enzymes like pyruvate kinase and phosphofructokinase, resulting in cells using the Krebs cycle instead of glycolysis and potential energy shortages (Li et al., 2020).

Given these results, would the potential benefits of directly incorporating carbon dots into food or edible food packaging/coating outweigh the potential risks? That is, is the prospect of extending shelf life and guaranteeing food quality and safety worth disrupting intestinal microflora and the gut/liver axis, inflammation, potential genetic mutations, and poor male reproduction? Of course, the consensus would be a resounding ‘no’, especially since there are much safer and proven methods available. Nevertheless, some research directly contradicts these negative findings. For instance, other studies suggest that carbon dots synthesised from food sources like citric acid/polyethylene polyamine and scrambled Coptidis Rhizoma (a herb used to prevent diarrhoea, alleviate stomach cramps, reduce fever, and treat dysentery) may in fact have positive health effects, and can help reduce the disease activity index score of colon tissue, prevent colon shortening, restore the gut microbial composition and the intestinal barrier, reduce gut inflammation, and help treat ulcerative colitis (Fig. 4, right) (Mou et al., 2023; Peng et al., 2024).

Currently, it remains unclear as to the exact effects carbon dots obtained from food may have on the body. With all these conflicting and ambiguous data, it is safe to say that it would be best to avoid their ingestion until significantly more studies supporting the beneficial effects emerge or, at the very least, research confirming the harmlessness of carbon dots obtained from biocompatible and non-toxic carbon sources, like food, is published. Therefore, at this moment, it is best if carbon dots are not included in edible packaging or added directly to food since the potential harmful effects drastically outweigh any benefits that may accompany their consumption. However, the incorporation of carbon dots into inedible food packaging for quality and safety assurance purposes is a direction worth pursuing.

The advantages associated with having carbon dots in direct contact with foodstuff is inarguable. Carbon dots have high surface area-to-volume ratio, which allows them to exhibit high activity and quickly react to environmental changes, making them ideal for real-time monitoring as well as good antioxidant and antimicrobial agents. These properties can be more fully exploited if carbon dots have contact with the foodstuff. However, it must be ensured that the carbon dots do not migrate from the packaging into the food itself. This migration is influenced by several factors, such as the physicochemical properties of the polymer matrix and matrix-nanoparticle interactions (Zabihzadeh Khajavi et al., 2019). High-molecular-weight polymers and polymer matrices with high degrees of crosslinking restrict particle movement and strong nanoparticle-polymer matrix interactions also decrease the chances of migration. Storage conditions also influence the potential leaching of nanoparticles into food. Temperature, exposure to light, and humidity affect the stability of nanoparticles within the polymer matrix and impact their migration behaviour. High temperatures increase nanoparticle movement, accelerating nanoparticle migration; exposure to light can degrade the polymer matrix, leading to the release of nanoparticles; and high-humidity environments can cause the polymer matrix to swell, creating larger spaces within the material, making it easier for nanoparticles to migrate (Zabihzadeh Khajavi et al., 2019).

Thus, a responsible approach to active and intelligent food packaging containing carbon dots would be to monitor the potential migration of these nanoparticles from the packaging into the food. Ideally, leaching should not occur at all; however, if it does, the amount of carbon dots released should be well below toxicity threshold levels. Therefore, if correct storage conditions are met, nanoparticle migration into foods would likely be trivial and not enough to cause harm. A study investigating the migration of silver nanoparticles showed that between 7.18 ± 1.28 and 74.72 ± 20.90 ng per cm2 of packaging surface area migrated into food (Adhikari et al., 2025). If carbon dots show similar results, this amount of leaching would fall significantly below the 1500 μg/mL toxicity threshold for zebrafish embryos or the 5 mg/kg of carbon dots that caused adverse effects in mice.

Another inherent risk of including nanoparticles in food packaging is their potential release into the environment during manufacturing, use, or disposal, which can affect environmental health. Carbon dots, especially those derived from biomass, are considered to be biodegradable; however, they are relatively stable and specific conditions must be met for degradation to occur. Studies have shown that carbon dots can exist for days within the environment; however, high temperatures, high pH, and prolonged exposure to ultraviolet light can accelerate their degradation (Y.-Y. Liu et al., 2021). Once in the environment, carbon dots can contaminate soil and water bodies and eventually enter the food chain. Plants and microalgae can act as potential entry points since they can absorb carbon dots via several processes (e.g., uptake and translocation, root and leaf absorption, translocation, and extracellular polymeric substances bridging) (Y. Liu et al., 2022). Because carbon dots can bioaccumulate in lower organisms, this can lead to the transfer of carbon dots to higher trophic levels in the food chain.

Since the potential for leaching into food or the environment is not zero, carbon dots designed for use in the food industry should be as harmless as possible. While they may not be completely non-toxic, less toxic carbon dots can be synthesised using certain protocols. Bottom-up methods using water or non-toxic organic solvents like ethanol, acetic acid, and dimethylsulphoxide are ideal for the fast, controlled synthesis of highly biocompatible carbon dots. The carbon source is also important, with organic sources being significantly less toxic than inorganic sources. As the majority of studies investigating the safety of carbon dots shows, biomass produces the least harmful particles and food is a good source of biopolymers and biomolecules.

Regardless of the possible safety measurements that may be undertaken to minimise potential health risks, the use of carbon dots, and nanoparticles in general, in the food industry remains controversial due to the unfamiliarity of consumers with such technology. However, policies and regulations focused on transparency and safety surrounding the use of nanotechnology have been adopted by several regulatory bodies. As it stands, these policies require manufacturers to disclose the use of nanoparticles for consumer awareness and that they conduct experiments on the safety of the nanoparticles being used and their potential migration into foodstuff. However, the sphere is still evolving and universal standardised testing methods and risk assessment frameworks are still being developed (Schoonjans et al., 2023). Currently, there are no globally adopted testing and characterisation methods, which presents a challenge for regulatory consistency. As of now, international food regulatory bodies like the European Food Safety Administration and the United States Department of Agriculture as well as the Food and Drug Administration assess nanotechnology designed for use in the food industry on a case-by-case basis (Control of Nanoscale Materials under the Toxic Substances Control Act, 2024; De Belder & House, 2011).

As the literature shows, the benefits of incorporating carbon dots into intelligent and active packaging and coatings is undeniable, and this practice has resulted in a much more involved approach to food quality and safety assurance. However, doing so results in carbon dots being in direct contact with foodstuffs, which might have significant detrimental health effects. Therefore, if included, it should be ensured that these carbon-based nanoparticles do not leach from the packaging into food intended for consumption or, if leaching does occur, the amount of carbon dots released is well below the minimum threshold for unfavourable health implications. Furthermore, packaging containing carbon dots should not be consumed and, instead, be washed off or otherwise removed. Using such an approach, the food industry can more effectively exploit the physicochemical properties of carbon dots without the potential harmful health effects.
This manuscript was written through the contribution of all authors.
Vitalina A. Malchenko – Visualisation, Writing – original draft.
Chantal T. Tracey – Conceptualisation, Visualisation, Writing – original draft, Supervision.
Elena F. Krivoshapkina – Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
Elena A. Nazarova – Conceptualisation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Russian Science Foundation Grant № 24-76-10093.
